title: Large-scale agentic quant research with Weights & Biases
transcript: [music] >> Hi. I'm Karan with Weights & Biases by Weights & Biases and today I'll talking about large-scale agentic quant research. I work very closely with quant desk and I hear the same thing everywhere. We can build models but we can't always trust them, reproduce them, or explain them to compliance. So today I'll show you how Weights & Biases solves for that with two use cases. An event-driven alpha research pipeline and a self-improving agent loop that optimizes its own strategy. Both fully traced, fully reproducible. So here's the architecture for our use case one. A market event comes in, let's say Nvidia Q4 earnings. The classifier then routes it to four specialized agents. The macro agent, the historical analogs, sentiment, and the quant agent. Each pulling in live data, making a bunch of tool calls for analysis. A synthesis agent then produces a probabilistic forecast. We then validate it against actual outcomes with six quant grade scorers like Brier score, log loss, hit rate, calibration error, etc. Let's take a look at this in the Weights & Biases UI. So what you're looking at right now is the Weights & Biases Weave workspace. Here, if I click into an individual trace, this is where you can see the Nvidia earning prediction. I can see all of my four agents. Across these agents, I can take a look at all the tools that have been called. For each and every tool call, I have a detailed analysis on the input output tokens, the total cost, as well as the latency. But let's take a look at the output here. Here I see my direction is neutral and the conviction is one out of 10. Which tells me the model does not trust its own forecast. And this is actually baffling, so I would want to understand why is this happening. Thankfully, I have Weights & Biases Weave enabled so it now gives me a complete traceability. I can drill into my historical agent and I can immediately see that my discover events tool call has returned zero analog or zero relevant analogs. It wasn't able to find any prior Nvidia earnings. So that's my root cause. In just one click, I can exactly see what's failing my actual model output. So what I can now do is I can easily flag this trace, maybe add an emoji, and then also say no historical fixed discover events. And all of this now gets logged as a feedback. Now what I can do is in my next iteration, I can start to fix this. So this is where I have my next iteration where I have actually gone ahead and tried to fix my discover events tool call. So this is where once I get into my discover events, I can now see that it's able to fetch far more relevant events. And with that one change, my forecast completely flips from neutral to bullish and the conviction jumps from one to five out of 10. So this is that one fix which is fully traceable before and after thanks to Weights & Biases Weave. Next, I want to validate this at scale. So this is where Weave evaluations let me run this across a curated data set of historical events with known outcomes. In this case, as I mentioned, the six metrics. So this is where I'm tracking all of them. So I have my Brier score, hit rate, profit factor, and all of those metrics that matter to a quant desk. I can easily filter it out by model versions, by data sets, and then easily compare these evaluations side by side. So let's say in this case, I want to compare my first three evals. So all I do is select and hit compare. This now gets me a radar plot. Let me filter out this plot to only those core metrics that I care the most about. I already have a view saved for it. So I can do is easily click on this view. And this is where I can start to pick my winner in very few seconds. I know that the lower values of Brier score, log loss get me a better performing event, a better performing model and a higher hit rate. In this case, the hit rate is the same across the three evals. But I can see my innermost eval, which is eval three, is clearly the winner when it comes to all of these core metrics. So this is where use case one gives me a research pipeline that I can debug and iterate on. But what if I don't want to manually tune my agent weights? What if I let the agents optimize themselves? So this is where we get into use case number two where we now have an automated optimization loop. I take a known historical event, run a swarm of parallel trials, each with different agent weight configurations, and then let a meta optimizer LLM adjust the weights to minimize, in this case, my Brier score. So think of this as a hyperparameter tuning workflow. But the hyperparameters are how much each research agent's signal contributes to the final forecast. This is where I use Weights & Biases Models to track my workflow. So in the same project, I can access my Weights & Biases Models UI, too. Once I click on my workspace, I can see all of these metrics that can be tracked in real time. In this case, let me expand onto this Brier score tracking. I can now start to see my this specific line, which had a very bad Brier score, almost .88, but it's starting to converge pretty well, getting me to a very good convergence, where I can see it converged to almost a .24 value, which is a pretty good convergence. So this is how I can start to track in real time all of these agents that are starting to run uh and starting to converge. Each and every trial is also logged as a step here. So this is where the parallel coordinates plot makes it very interesting for quants. So in this particular parallel coordinates plot, all of my vertical axes that you see are the agent weights and the rightmost column, which is Brier score here, is the final metric that I want to get the best value out of. What I can do is since I know lower the Brier score value, better is the performance, I can quickly filter it out to the lower values of Brier score. So in my case, I can start to see a very clear strategy. The best strategies push the weights to the extreme rather than hedging in the middle. Here you can see all of my weights which gave me the best Brier score value are all the extreme rather than the indecisive middle. This is a very clear insight, which would have taken me a lot of time if I did not have this particular visual. Thanks to Weights & Biases Models, I can now make those observations with just one click of a button. And because Weights & Biases Weave and Weights & Biases Models are tightly integrated, I can now click into any trial and actually see the full agent trace, which now tells me their exact thinking process, which weights shifted, why the optimizer made that decision, so I have full provenance from strategy to outcome. So let's say if I click on this one example trace, in here, I can start to see the entire thinking process, the entire strategy, which actually led to the generation of a better performing model, a better prior score in my example. So, what you're seeing is a full cycle, a swarm of agents exploring the weights, weights and biases models, tracking each and every trial in real time, and Weights & Biases Weave capturing the reasoning behind each and every one. The parallel coordinates plot tells me what is working, and agent that traces tells me why. Like, why is this working? This is the same iterative optimization loop quants already run today for factor models, but now applied to LLM agent pipelines with full traceability across both the experiment tracking and the agent behavior. So, to recap, Weights & Biases Weave gives you end-to-end traceability from hypothesis to backtesting to decision and compliance grade reproducibility, as well as having the ability to compare and optimize strategies at scale. Thanks for watching.
